chunk_summary: Appetite testing is a standardized tool to determine outpatient eligibility for children with SAM, ensuring proper triage between uncomplicated and complicated cases based on specific intake thresholds and clinical criteria.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of appetite testing in differentiating uncomplicated and complicated SAM cases.
Learn the procedural steps to conduct appetite testing and avoid false results.
Identify minimum intake thresholds for therapeutic feeds based on weight bands.
Interpret appetite test results and link them to appropriate treatment pathways.
key_facts: Appetite testing is performed when a child meets SAM criteria: WHZ/WLZ < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm, or nutritional oedema.
Testing should occur in a quiet area, with no food intake for 2 hours prior, and up to 60 minutes allowed for feeding.
Minimum intake thresholds: <4 kg = 15 g, 4–7 kg = 25 g, 7–10 kg = 33–35 g, 10–14.9 kg = 50 g of therapeutic feed.
Pass: Outpatient care is acceptable if no danger signs or severe complications exist and follow-up is feasible.
Fail: Refer/admit for inpatient stabilization and repeat testing during follow-up if weight gain is static or falling.
reference_quote: "Perform an appetite test when a child meets SAM criteria (WHZ/WLZ < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm in children 6–59 months, or nutritional oedema) and is stable enough to attempt feeding."
document_key_topics: Minimum intake thresholds for therapeutic feeds
Criteria for outpatient versus inpatient care in SAM
